summary:
The Fall of the Qin Dynasty (210–206 BC) came with remarkable speed: just four years after the death of [[event:3141|Qin Shi Huang]], the empire he had created through decades of conquest collapsed under the weight of peasant rebellions, aristocratic uprisings, and the incompetence of his successors, leading directly to the [[event:1124|Chu-Han Contention]] that eventually produced the [[event:77|Han Dynasty]].

Qin Shi Huang died in 210 BC while on an inspection tour. The court eunuch Zhao Gao and chancellor Li Si suppressed news of the death and forged an edict ordering the rightful heir Fusu to commit suicide, installing the pliable Ershi Huangdi instead. Zhao Gao then systematically eliminated potential rivals through false accusations, eventually including Li Si himself. Meanwhile, the empire's harsh labor requirements — the [[event:1121|Great Wall]], canals, palaces, and the emperor's tomb — had driven millions of peasants to desperation. A rebellion by two conscript laborers, Chen Sheng and Wu Guang, in 209 BC ignited a general uprising. Former aristocrats of the conquered states — especially Chu — raised armies, and within months the Qin armies were facing coordinated resistance. The last Qin emperor surrendered to Liu Bang (the future [[event:1136|Liu Bang - Emperor Gaozu of Han]]) in 206 BC.

read_more_links.title: Chen Sheng and Wu Guang rebellion
